es.literal: God willing (from Arabic law šaʾa llāh)
es.meaning: I hope so; if only — a wish particle that pulls the subjunctive.

it.meaning: If only; I wish — and, separately, 'maybe'.

bridge.note: Two wish-particles, each a fossil of a different empire: ojalá is Arabic ('if God wills'), magari is Greek ('blessed'). The two peninsulas' histories compressed into one word each. Both pull the subjunctive — but magari moonlights as 'maybe' (magari vengo domani = maybe I'll come), a job ojalá never does.

interference.es_to_it: Hearing magari as pure ojalá misparses half its uses: 'magari vengo domani' is 'maybe I'll come', not a wish. Context and mood decide — indicative magari is 'maybe', subjunctive magari is a wish.
interference.it_to_es: Deploying ojalá to mean 'maybe' fails — Spanish splits the jobs: quizá / a lo mejor for maybe, ojalá only for wishes.

review.notes: Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14. Etymology pair (Arabic vs Greek) is a card-front gift. Exclamative 'Magari!' = 'I wish!' worth a reviewer check as third sense.
